data.title: European Stocks Close Mostly Higher in Tuesday Trading; EU Touts India Trade Deal
data.description:
12:06 PM EST, 01/27/2026 (MT Newswires) -- The European stock markets closed mostly higher in Tuesday trading as the Stoxx Europe gained 0.59%, the FTSE 100 rose 0.58%, France's CAC was up 0.27%, and the Swiss Market Index increased 0.56%, while Germany's DAX was off 0.1%.

In economic news, European Commission President Ursula von der Leyen described a new trade deal between the European Union and India as a "tale of two giants who choose partnership." The agreement will integrate the EU-India supply chains, cut tariffs and create jobs for "millions of workers," claimed von der Leyen.

In Spain, the unemployment rate declined to 9.93% in Q4 from 10.45% the previous quarter, according to the Spanish Statistical Institute. The number of unemployed declined by 136,100 to 2.48 million. It was the country's lowest unemployment rate since 2008, according to media reports.

The European Commission said Tuesday that it has launched two separate lines of proceedings to assist Alphabet to comply with its obligations under the Digital Markets Act.

The regulator said the proceedings "formalize" the commission's dialogue with Google, the regulator said.

The commission said it will end the proceedings within six months and will communicate preliminary findings to Google that set out proposed measures it intends to impose on Google, which could include penalty payments, within three months.

Google's Senior Competition Counsel Clare Kelly told MT Newswires that Google is already licensing Search data to competitors under the DMA. "We are concerned that further rules which are often driven by competitor grievances, rather than the interest of consumers, will compromise user privacy, security, and innovation."

And in corporate news, Shell, Eni, and their partners at the Karachaganak oil field in Kazakhstan lost an international arbitration case and face compensation payments to the government of as much as $4 billion, Bloomberg reported Monday, citing people familiar with the matter.

"We are not able to comment on the arbitration until a final decision is made and until such time, any figures circulating are speculative," Eni told MT Newswires via email. "As the proceedings continue before the arbitral tribunal, we remain bound by the confidentiality requirements of the arbitration rules."

Shell didn't reply to a request for comment from MT Newswires.

Shares of Shell gained 0.9% in London, while Eni's shares were up 1% in Milan.

UBS has asked a US court to dismiss a lawsuit filed by a former trader who accuses the bank of sacrificing him to prosecutors during the Libor rigging scandal to protect senior executives, Bloomberg reported Tuesday.

Lawyers for the Swiss bank told a Connecticut court that claims by the trader, Tom Hayes, were "spurious" and amounted to "forum shopping," noting that Hayes is an English citizen who was tried and convicted in the UK, according to the report.

Hayes sued UBS in October for "malicious prosecution," seeking at least $400 million, alleging the bank "scapegoated" him and ruined his life, Bloomberg reported.

UBS did not immediately respond to MT Newswires' request for comment.

Shares of UBS rose 1.1% in Zurich.

Nokia signed a non-binding agreement with Blaize to jointly create and deploy artificial intelligence systems for real-world operations, the latter said Tuesday.

The partnership focuses on combining network automation and cloud infrastructure with specialized hardware to enable AI that runs at the edge of networks, Blaize said.

Shares of Nokia increased 1.7% in Helsinki.

Bank stocks trended higher in Tuesday trading as HSBC and NatWest rose 2.7% and 2.5% respectively in London, followed by Barclays and Lloyds Banking, which were up 1.9% and 1.8% respectively. Credit Agricole and BNP Paribas gained 2.5% and 1.6% respectively in Paris, while Societe Generale was up 1.4%. And in Frankfurt, Commerzbank was up 2.2%.
